topic: Pattern-interrupt cadence and visual-novelty pacing in top explainer content
caveat: Highest confidence: peer-reviewed Hollywood shot-length study (PMC3485803) and the leaked MrBeast production handbook. Kurzgesagt/Infographics Show/Johnny Harris findings rest on process breakdowns (med).

findings.claim: Hollywood average shot length fell from ~10s (1930s-40s) to below 4s after 2000 (r=-.75, 160 films measured); motion per shot rose in parallel.
High-performing short-form averages one cut every 2-4 seconds; a top-100-Shorts analysis found 2.5s average clip length correlated with ~35% higher completion than 4s+.
The 5-second rule: never a 5-second block where nothing on screen changes. Niche pacing: B2B every 8-10s, Gen Z entertainment every 2-3s.
A pattern interrupt buys ~5 more seconds of watch time each; place one at 25-35s where new viewers drift.
Fixed-cadence interrupts habituate ('the brain detects the pattern and stops reacting'); jitter intervals, and cap stacking (deliberate disruption, not digital whiplash).
MrBeast: the first minute is the most important; front-load the best visuals/music/effects; typical videos lose ~21M viewers in the opening 60s.
MrBeast structures re-hooks at fixed beats (~min 3, ~min 6/halfway); back-half viewers watch semi-passively and tolerate longer holds.
Micro-hooks/cliffhangers every 30-60s ('that's not even the crazy part'); all dead air removed.

Kurzgesagt: all animation timed to the voiceover; every on-screen element animated manually so secondary/background motion is essentially continuous; per-video original score with deep booms on key words; split-second easter-egg gags as micro-rewards.
Infographics Show sustains long runtimes with a fixed template: recurring silhouettes/icon sets, motion-graphic data reveals, short clearly-labeled segments - not editing gimmicks.
Johnny Harris designs visuals FIRST (script serves visuals) and layers texture (film burns, light leaks, grain) over nearly every static asset; three-act arc with an explicit promise restated.

interrupt_taxonomy.name: zoom-punch
interrupt_taxonomy.use: emphasis on key word/stat; jittered, never metronomic

interrupt_taxonomy.name: b-roll-flash
interrupt_taxonomy.use: 1-2s cutaway insert for novelty without changing the argument

interrupt_taxonomy.name: stat-card-kinetic-text
interrupt_taxonomy.use: every key number rendered on screen synced to the spoken word

interrupt_taxonomy.name: scale-reveal
interrupt_taxonomy.use: pull back to recontextualize scale; the wow beat, one per major section

interrupt_taxonomy.name: reaction-insert
interrupt_taxonomy.use: re-hook / comic relief via character reaction or aside

interrupt_taxonomy.name: sound-accent
interrupt_taxonomy.use: reinforces nearly every visual interrupt; silence itself is an interrupt

interrupt_taxonomy.name: ambient-motion-texture
interrupt_taxonomy.use: keeps quiet narration stretches alive between hard interrupts

actionable_rules: Never a 5-second block with no on-screen change; for our 2.5D explainer target a new visual idea every 4-8s with beats every <5s as the hard floor.
Jitter interrupt spacing; never the same interrupt type on a fixed interval 3+ times.
First 10s: highest visual-change density of the piece; payoff-promise stated and shown.
Explicit re-hook at 25-35s, then near the 1/2 mark.
Every stat/number renders as an on-screen card synced to the spoken word.
Sound accent paired with every major visual interrupt.
Continuous secondary motion during narration stretches with no hard cut - no fully static asset.
Max 2 simultaneous interrupt types in any ~1s window.
At least one scale-reveal per major section, aligned to narrative escalation.
Section boundaries get a high-salience interrupt (palette flip or whip-pan + label card).
Escalate density in the front half; slightly longer holds allowed in the back half, never below the 5s floor.
